questions.question: Article 40.4.1° of the Constitution guarantees that no citizen shall be deprived of personal liberty save 'in accordance with law'. In The State (Burke) v Lennon [1940] IR 136, Gavan Duffy J held that this phrase means:

options.A: Any detention authorised by an Act of the Oireachtas is automatically lawful
options.B: Detention must not only have a statutory basis but must also comply with the constitutional requirements of justice and fair procedures
options.C: Only a court order can authorise a lawful deprivation of personal liberty
options.D: Detention in accordance with law requires the consent of the Attorney General

questions.explanation: In The State (Burke) v Lennon [1940] IR 136, Gavan Duffy J articulated the principle that 'in accordance with law' in Art. 40.4.1° means more than having a statutory basis — it requires that the law authorising detention be constitutionally valid and that the detention be consistent with the constitutional requirements of justice. A detention authorised by an unconstitutional statute is not 'in accordance with law'. This expansive interpretation transformed the habeas corpus provision into a powerful constitutional check on the executive's detention powers.

questions.question: Under Article 40.4.2°, who may apply to the High Court for an inquiry into the legality of a person's detention?

options.A: Only the detained person themselves, through a legal representative
options.B: Any person acting on behalf of the detained person, or the detained person themselves
options.C: Only the Attorney General on behalf of the State
options.D: Only persons with a legal interest in the detained person, such as family members

questions.explanation: Article 40.4.2° expressly allows any person to apply on behalf of the detained individual — or the detained person themselves — for an inquiry into the legality of the detention. This is deliberately broad: it enables family members, friends, solicitors or any concerned person to bring a habeas corpus application. This reflects the fundamental importance of the remedy and addresses the practical reality that a detained person may be unable to initiate proceedings personally.

questions.question: Article 40.4.2° requires the High Court, upon an application, to 'forthwith' inquire into the legality of the detention and 'order the release of such person unless satisfied that he is being detained in accordance with the law'. This procedural requirement is:

options.A: Directory only — the court may delay inquiry if the calendar is busy
options.B: Mandatory — the court must inquire urgently; any delay would be constitutionally impermissible
options.C: Subject to judicial discretion as to timing based on the gravity of the alleged illegality
options.D: Mandatory only where the detained person is facing criminal charges

questions.explanation: The word 'forthwith' in Art. 40.4.2° is mandatory, not directory. The High Court must act immediately upon receiving a habeas corpus application — the urgency of the constitutional right to liberty demands it. This is reinforced by the gravity of the right at stake. The court has a duty to act promptly; any avoidable delay in conducting the inquiry would be constitutionally impermissible. If the court is not satisfied that the detention is lawful, it must order release.

questions.question: In The State (Royle) v Kelly [1974] IR 259, the Supreme Court developed the distinction between 'initial invalidity' and 'subsequent invalidity' in detention. What is the significance of this distinction?

options.A: Only initially invalid detentions can be challenged; subsequently invalid detentions are immune from habeas corpus
options.B: A detention that was initially lawful can become unlawful if its legal basis is subsequently removed (e.g., if the statute authorising it is declared unconstitutional); in that case, the person must be released
options.C: Initially invalid detention cannot be cured by subsequent compliance with procedures
options.D: The distinction affects only the quantum of damages available, not the right to release

questions.explanation: The State (Royle) v Kelly [1974] IR 259 established that the validity of detention is assessed at the time the habeas corpus application is heard, not only at the time of arrest. A detention that began lawfully may become unlawful if the statutory basis is subsequently struck down, or if procedural requirements are subsequently breached. This ensures that the Art. 40.4.2° inquiry is a present-tense assessment of legality, not merely a retrospective review of the initial arrest.

questions.question: Under the Offences Against the State Act 1939, s.30, a person may be detained for questioning without charge for up to 24 hours (extendable). This was challenged in The People (DPP) v Quilligan [1986] IR 495. What was the outcome?

options.A: The Supreme Court struck down s.30 as an unconstitutional deprivation of liberty without judicial warrant
options.B: The Supreme Court upheld s.30 as a constitutionally permitted limitation on liberty in the interest of public order and security, provided the detention for questioning was genuinely carried out for that purpose
options.C: The Supreme Court held s.30 was constitutional only where the detained person was also charged within 24 hours
options.D: The Supreme Court referred the question of s.30’s constitutionality to the President under Article 26

questions.explanation: In People (DPP) v Quilligan [1986] IR 495, the Supreme Court upheld s.30 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 as constitutionally valid. Detention for questioning under s.30 is a constitutionally permissible limitation on personal liberty in the interests of public order and security — a legitimate objective. However, the detention must be genuinely for investigative purposes, not a pretext. If the Gardaí have no genuine intention of using the detention for questioning, the detention may be unlawful.

questions.question: The High Court in a habeas corpus application finds that a prisoner's detention is lawful on the face of the warrant but the prisoner alleges that the Gardaí breached his rights during arrest. What is the court's approach?

options.A: The court must release the prisoner immediately upon any allegation of rights breach during arrest
options.B: The court distinguishes between the validity of the detention order and collateral matters — unless the rights breach goes to the legality of the detention itself, the prisoner remains detained and must seek other remedies
options.C: The court orders an automatic stay of the detention pending investigation of the rights breach
options.D: The court refers the rights breach allegation to the Garda Ombudsman before proceeding

questions.explanation: Article 40.4.2° proceedings are focused on the current legality of detention, not on all matters relating to its circumstances. Where a detention order is valid on its face and the alleged rights breach is collateral (e.g., a breach of rights during interrogation that does not affect the lawfulness of the remand in custody), the court will not order release on that basis. Other remedies (damages in plenary action; exclusion of evidence at trial) are available. The test is whether the detention itself is in accordance with law.

questions.question: Article 40.4.6° provides that the Article 40.4 habeas corpus procedure is in addition to any other proceedings a court has under the Constitution or at common law to inquire into detention. This preserves:

options.A: The statutory right of appeal from detention decisions under the Criminal Justice Act 1984
options.B: The common law writ of habeas corpus alongside the constitutional procedure, giving the court flexibility to use either or both
options.C: The Attorney General’s power to independently review Garda detention decisions
options.D: The District Court’s exclusive jurisdiction to review pre-trial detention

questions.explanation: Article 40.4.6° preserves the pre-existing common law writ of habeas corpus alongside the Article 40.4.2° constitutional inquiry. Courts may use either procedure or both. In practice, the constitutional procedure under Art. 40.4.2° has largely superseded the common law writ, but the preservation clause ensures flexibility and avoids any argument that the constitutional provision was intended to be the exclusive remedy.

questions.question: In The People (DPP) v Healy [1990] 2 IR 73, the Supreme Court held that a suspect had a constitutional right to consult a solicitor before and during police questioning. The constitutional basis for this right is:

options.A: Article 38.1 (trial in due course of law) and the right to a fair trial
options.B: Article 40.4.1° (personal liberty) and the right to have detention lawful by reference to constitutional standards, including the right to legal advice during police custody
options.C: Article 40.3.1° (personal rights) encompassing the right to legal representation at all stages
options.D: Both A and B — the constitutional right to consult a solicitor derives from both the right to a fair trial and the requirement that detention be constitutionally valid

questions.explanation: People (DPP) v Healy [1990] 2 IR 73 (Finlay CJ) held that a detained person has a constitutional right to consult a solicitor without delay. The right derives from both Art. 40.4 (the lawfulness of detention requires respect for the detainee's constitutional rights) and Art. 38.1 (the right to a fair trial requires that evidence not be obtained in deliberate violation of constitutional rights). Breach of this right may lead to exclusion of statements obtained in its violation.

questions.question: Article 40.4.3° provides that where the body of a person is produced before the High Court in habeas corpus proceedings and the court orders release, no court shall subsequently direct that the person be detained for the same reason. This is known as the:

options.B: Once-off nature of habeas corpus — the res judicata principle as applied to Article 40.4
options.C: Principle of double jeopardy in detention

questions.explanation: Article 40.4.3° provides that once the High Court has ordered release in a habeas corpus inquiry, no other court may re-detain the person for the same reason. This is a form of res judicata — the legality of the detention on that ground has been conclusively determined. It prevents the State from simply re-arresting a person on the same basis after an Art. 40.4 release order. The person may be re-arrested on different grounds or pursuant to a valid court order.

questions.question: A person is detained under a warrant that is valid on its face. Their solicitor discovers that the Garda who signed the s.30 detention order had no authorisation to do so under the Offences Against the State Act 1939. This is argued in Article 40.4.2° proceedings. What is the most likely outcome?

options.A: The court will refuse to look behind a warrant that is valid on its face
options.B: The court will order release because the detention lacks a valid legal basis — the unlawfulness goes to the legality of the detention itself
options.C: The court will adjourn to allow the Garda to obtain retrospective authorisation
options.D: The court will refer the matter to the District Court for determination

questions.explanation: Where a procedural requirement going to the legality of the detention itself has not been complied with (e.g., a s.30 extension signed by an officer without authority), the detention is not 'in accordance with law' under Art. 40.4.1°. The High Court in Art. 40.4.2° proceedings will look beyond the face of the warrant to the underlying legality, and if the procedural requirement was a condition of lawful detention (not merely directory), the court will order release. This is consistent with DPP v Byrne [1987] IR 363.

questions.question: Article 28.3.3° of the Constitution permits the Oireachtas to suspend Art. 40.4.2° during a national emergency. Which constitutional provision deals with the emergency detention powers and what safeguards apply?

options.A: The suspension requires a presidential proclamation approved by referendum within thirty days
options.B: The emergency powers legislation must be approved by a two-thirds majority of the Dáil and by the Seanad
options.C: Emergency legislation suspending habeas corpus must be passed by both Houses of the Oireachtas and must, under Art. 28.3.3°, be expressed to be for the purpose of securing public safety during the emergency
options.D: Only the Supreme Court may order suspension of habeas corpus during a national emergency declared by the Government

questions.explanation: Article 28.3.3° provides that nothing in the Constitution (including Art. 40.4.2° habeas corpus) shall be invoked to invalidate emergency legislation passed in time of war or armed rebellion. Such legislation must be expressed to be for the purpose of securing public safety and preserving the State, and must be passed by both Houses of the Oireachtas. This was used to uphold emergency internment provisions under the Offences Against the State (Amendment) Act 1940 during the Emergency.

questions.question: In JJ Harte (Deceased) v McEllin [2018] IEHC 41, the High Court considered a habeas corpus application by a person detained under the Mental Health Act 2001. The court emphasised that the 'in accordance with law' requirement under Art. 40.4.1° in the context of involuntary psychiatric detention means:

options.A: Any recommendation by a registered medical practitioner is sufficient to make the detention lawful
options.B: The procedural safeguards under the Mental Health Act 2001 (psychiatrist certification, tribunal review, patient representation) are not just directory but mandatory conditions of lawful detention
options.C: Involuntary psychiatric detention is exempt from Art. 40.4 because it does not engage personal liberty
options.D: The 'in accordance with law' test cannot be applied to clinical decisions made by medical practitioners

questions.explanation: Irish courts have consistently held that the procedural safeguards in the Mental Health Act 2001 — independent psychiatric examination, Mental Health Tribunal review within 21 days, right to legal representation — are mandatory conditions of lawful involuntary detention, not mere administrative formalities. Where these safeguards are not observed, the detention is not 'in accordance with law' within Art. 40.4.1° and the person must be released. See also MX v Health Service Executive [2012] 3 IR 254.

questions.question: What is the constitutional significance of The State (Quinn) v Ryan [1965] IR 70 in the context of the Article 40.4 habeas corpus procedure?

options.A: The Supreme Court held that Art. 40.4 cannot be used to challenge a lawful extradition warrant
options.B: The Supreme Court struck down the practice of using Article 40.4 proceedings to test the legality of detention on remand in a District Court
options.C: The Supreme Court asserted that the High Court’s Art. 40.4 jurisdiction cannot be ousted or undermined by any statutory provision, even one purporting to make detention unreviewable
options.D: The Supreme Court held that Art. 40.4 habeas corpus proceedings are available only in the High Court, not the Supreme Court or Circuit Court

questions.explanation: The State (Quinn) v Ryan [1965] IR 70 is a landmark case asserting the constitutional primacy of Art. 40.4. The Supreme Court held that no statutory provision can oust or undermine the High Court’s Article 40.4 jurisdiction to inquire into the legality of detention. Any attempt to make a detention unreviewable by statute would itself be unconstitutional. The constitutional habeas corpus remedy is inviolable — it cannot be removed or limited by ordinary legislation.

questions.question: A person is remanded in custody following a lawful arrest and charge. Their solicitor argues that the conditions of the remand prison violate the person's constitutional right to bodily integrity (Art. 40.3.1°). Can Article 40.4.2° proceedings be used to secure release on this basis?

options.A: Yes — any violation of constitutional rights during detention renders the detention unlawful for Art. 40.4.2° purposes
options.B: No — the legality of the underlying detention is the subject of Art. 40.4.2° proceedings; conditions of detention give rise to separate remedies (injunction, judicial review, plenary action) but do not affect the legality of the remand order itself
options.C: Yes — the Supreme Court in Cahill v Governor of Mountjoy Prison [2012] held that prison conditions automatically render detention unlawful
options.D: No — prisoners forfeit all constitutional rights upon conviction and remand

questions.explanation: Article 40.4.2° proceedings are directed at the legality of the detention itself — the lawfulness of the order or legal basis for custody. Complaints about the conditions of detention (overcrowding, inhumane conditions, inadequate medical care) while potentially implicating Art. 40.3 bodily integrity do not render the underlying remand order unlawful. Such complaints must be pursued through judicial review, injunction, or plenary action. See Brennan v Governor of Portlaoise Prison [1999] 1 ILRM 190.
